input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.content: Good — both .NET 9 and .NET 10 runtimes are available. Let me read the source files.
Now I'll create the benchmark project targeting both .NET 9 and .NET 10.
data.reasoningText:
Now I need to create a BenchmarkDotNet project that benchmarks AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount across .NET 9 and .NET 10. I'll create a separate benchmark project that references the TextAnalyzer source and targets both net9.0 and net10.0.

Let me create a benchmark project that:
1. Multi-targets net9.0 and net10.0
2. Uses BenchmarkDotNet
3. Benchmarks the three methods

I'll include the TextAnalyzer.cs source directly in the benchmark project to avoid multi-targeting issues with the library project.

toolCalls.name: create

toolResult.sessionLog:

create file mode 100644
index 0000000..0000000
--- a/dev/null
+++ b/tmp/sv-3b6c8b0a05494152922daeb47be2ef64/TextAnalyzer.Benchmarks/TextAnalyzer.Benchmarks.csproj
@@ -1,0 +1,13 @@
+<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
+    <TargetFrameworks>net9.0;net10.0</TargetFrameworks>
+    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
+    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
+  </PropertyGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>
+    <PackageReference Include="BenchmarkDotNet" Version="0.14.*" />
+    <Compile Include="../TextAnalyzer/TextAnalyzer.cs" Link="TextAnalyzer.cs" />
+  </ItemGroup>
+</Project>
+

data.content: Need to fix the runtime specification. Let me use the CLI `--runtimes` approach instead:
data.reasoningText:
CoreRuntime.Core100 doesn't exist in the installed version of BenchmarkDotNet. Let me use `CoreRuntime.CreateForNewVersion("net10.0", ".NET 10.0")` or use the moniker-based approach. Let me check what's available.

Actually, I can use `CoreRuntime.CreateForNewVersion` - but I need to check the API. Alternatively, I'll use the `[SimpleJob]` attribute with custom runtime configuration.

Actually, the easiest approach with BenchmarkDotNet when it doesn't know about a new runtime yet is to create it manually: `new CoreRuntime(new RuntimeMoniker(), "net10.0", ".NET 10.0", "10.0")` or similar. But the exact constructor may vary.
 
Since we're multi-targeting, BenchmarkDotNet should automatically detect the runtime from the target frameworks, so I can skip the manual Job configuration entirely. The simplest solution is to pass the runtimes directly via command line with `--runtimes net9.0 net10.0`, which BenchmarkDotNet supports natively.

data.content: BenchmarkDotNet doesn't know about .NET 10 yet. Let me use the custom runtime API:
data.reasoningText:
BenchmarkDotNet 0.14.x doesn't know about .NET 10 yet. I need a different approach. I'll configure jobs manually using `CoreRuntime.CreateForNewVersion` or similar.

Let me check what API is available. Actually, I can create a custom runtime. Let me look at the BenchmarkDotNet API for creating custom runtimes.

The approach would be to create a custom config:
```csharp
var net10 = CoreRuntime.CreateForNewVersion("net10.0", ".NET 10.0");
```

Or I could just use `Job.Default.WithRuntime(CoreRuntime.Core90)` for .NET 9 and manually create the .NET 10 runtime.


Let me test the `CreateForNewVersion` method to see if it works for defining .NET 10.

data.content: BenchmarkDotNet 0.14 doesn't support .NET 10. Let me check for a newer version or use a manual approach:
data.reasoningText:
BenchmarkDotNet 0.14 doesn't support .NET 10 at all. Let me try a newer version of BenchmarkDotNet, or use a different approach entirely. Let me check the latest available BenchmarkDotNet version - maybe there's a nightly/preview that supports .NET 10.

Actually, let me try a simpler approach. Instead of using BenchmarkDotNet (which has compatibility issues with .NET 10), I'll write a manual benchmark harness that runs on each runtime. This is simpler and will work reliably.

Actually wait - let me check if there's a newer version of BenchmarkDotNet. 

0.15 might exist.

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "dotnet package search BenchmarkDotNet --exact-match --take 1 2>&1 | head -20", "description": "Check latest BenchmarkDotNet version", "initial_wait": 15}
